narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model, trained on data from thousands of past students, estimated a 96% chance that you would not pass the bar exam on your first try—a risk level that led to the rejection to protect the school's bar passage rates. This prediction stemmed largely from your academic rankings in school, particularly your placement in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years, which stood out as the strongest signals pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Your Year 3 decile rank had the single biggest negative impact, far below the much higher averages (around the 6th decile) seen among students who went on to pass the bar. Similarly, your LSAT score of 24 played a major role in tipping the scales against you, as it was substantially lower than the typical 37-plus scores of successful bar takers. Even your Year 1 decile rank in the bottom tier added to the concern, diverging from the stronger early performances common among those who passed. While factors like your middle-class family income aligned reasonably with many who succeeded—where middle and upper-middle brackets dominate—and your choice of full-time study matched the norm for passers, they weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Compared to admitted students, whose profiles typically feature top-half deciles, LSATs in the mid-to-high 30s, and undergrad GPAs around 3.24 versus your 2.60, your combination painted a picture of higher risk in the model's eyes. These key academic indicators weren't just slightly off; they were the dominant forces driving the high failure probability for your specific situation. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your undergraduate background and full-time commitment showing real dedication. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, as even a jump toward the 37 average could reshape future predictions. Strengthening your recent grades or pursuing targeted bar prep courses might also help demonstrate growth. Reach out to admissions advisors for personalized advice on reapplying after addressing these core areas—they've seen students turn similar profiles around.
